title: Edge client proxy config sketch
preview:
Purpose:
Quick personal sketch for an "edge" client-facing proxy that translates lightweight client requests into Redwood inference routes while handling auth, caching, and graceful fallbacks. Meant as a starting point for a small shim that runs near customers (edge or local) to reduce tails and enforce routing poli...
text:
Purpose:
Quick personal sketch for an "edge" client-facing proxy that translates lightweight client requests into Redwood inference routes while handling auth, caching, and graceful fallbacks. Meant as a starting point for a small shim that runs near customers (edge or local) to reduce tails and enforce routing policies.

Design goals (high level):
- minimal client SDK changes (thin edge library)
- local/pinned routing: prefer region-local Redwood Dedicated when available
- offline-safe: queue + backoff for transient network issues
- transparent telemetry: attach trace-ids + token usage metadata for cost attribution
- configurable policy surface (per-app or per-route)

Non-goals:
- full model orchestration (that stays on Redwood control plane)
- heavy stateful model caching beyond short-lived KV for prefixes

Quick ASCII diagram:

 [Client app] -> [Edge proxy shim (client)] -> HTTPS -...

Quick notes / assumptions

- “Coverage” means: for any sensitive action, at least one v1 event emitted with correct actor + target + tenancy + correlation IDs. (See PM-938 / ENG-4828 work.)
- Delivery semantics: best-effort at producer; pipeline aims at-least-once w/ buffering + retry; duplicates possible (customer guidance: de...

text:
Context
- Spike goal: figure out where SLO evaluation belongs (controller vs separate service), draft an adapter interface to query SLO status for a rollout scope (route/model version/region/tier), and enumerate failure modes.
- Intended actions: pause rollout OR rollback (later) when breach confirmed, with guardrails to prevent flapping.
- Inputs from SRE: dual-window burn-rate, min sample size, staleness detection, and “don’t rollback during known incident” override.

Assumptions / terms (for this doc)
- “Rollout scope” = (service/route) + candidate model version + region + customer tier + rollout_id.
- Baseline = currently serving model version / control group.
- Candidate = new model version in canary or A/B.
- Monitor provider = slo-toolkit (burn-rate endpoint) + underlying metrics store.

Early architecture sketch (text)

text:
Context
- Project: workload-aware batching defaults with 3 latency SLO tiers: fast / standard / cost-efficient.
- Platform question: how do customers (and internal routing/policy) select a tier, and how does it interact with existing config knobs and Dedicated overrides.
- This doc is a working set of notes for API surface + policy evaluation order. Not a final spec.

Goals for the API/config surface
- Simple for Hosted API: customers can choose a tier without learning scheduler knobs.
- Safe default: if customer does nothing, we pick a tier (likely “standard”) and can change the underlying knob set over time.
- Clear override precedence: avoid “why didn’t my setting take effect?” tickets.
- Backwards compatible: existing customers with manual batching configs should behave the same until they opt into tier defaults (or we migrate with a flag + explicit comms).
- Works across deployme...

text:
Purpose:
This snapshot is a concise customer-facing reference describing the shape, retention, and export options for Redwood Inference audit telemetry. It is intended for security reviewers and customers completing security questionnaires who want a single-page technical summary of: what events we capture, which fields are available, how RBAC v2 decisions are recorded, and how telemetry can be exported to customer SIEMs or used with our evidence automation tooling.

High-level summary:
- What we record: authentication events, authorization checks (RBAC v2), model invocation metadata (model id, version, runtime variant), request/response metadata (status, latency, token counts), quota and rate-limit hits, deployment lifecycle events (rollouts, instance restarts), and system-level alerts (OOM, GPU faults). We do NOT log customer prompt text by default in hosted deployments; request/res...

title: Throughput Repro Lab Journal and Experiment Matrix
preview:

- This doc is a running journal + compact experiment matrix to capture configurations that consistently rep...
text:
Purpose and quick summary:
- Running a compact lab notebook that prioritizes reproducible throughput numbers while uncovering brittle failure modes (thermal throttling, KV cache pressure, batching edge cases).
- This doc is a running journal + compact experiment matrix to capture configurations that consistently reproduce behavior across infra (devbox -> staging -> dedicated pool).

Top-level hypothesis to test:
- Variability in peak throughput often correlates with: client-side pacing jitter, tokenization variance in sample corpus, and priming order for KV caches. If we attack all three systematically we can reduce run-to-run 95th percentile throughput variance to <4%.

Core definitions used in experiments:
- Steady-state window: 3 minute sliding window after warmup ends used for baseline metrics.
- Warmup phase: traffic pattern that ramps from 0 to target qps over a configurable dur...

title: KV Lease Epochs and Priority Lanes — Throttling & Prefill/Decode Coordination (Draft)
preview:
Summary:
This note proposes an "epochal lease" model for KV residency combined with explicit priority lanes for streams to better coordinate overlapping prefill and decode work. The goal is to reduce tail latency regressions during mixed workloads (short chat vs long prefill-heavy requests) while minimizing throughp...
text:
Summary:
This note proposes an "epochal lease" model for KV residency combined with explicit priority lanes for streams to better coordinate overlapping prefill and decode work. The goal is to reduce tail latency regressions during mixed workloads (short chat vs long prefill-heavy requests) while minimizing throughput loss from conservative throttling.

Motivation / problem statement:
- Current prefilling + decode overlap sometimes causes long-running prefill work to evict hot KV entries or grab GPU kernels for extended periods, increasing tail latency for streaming decodes.
- Existing heuristics (simple TTLs, token-count quotas) are brittle as request mixes shift. We need a more adaptive mechanism that encodes intent (short interactive vs long batch) and gives the serving runtime levers to schedule microbatches, KV residency, and kernel affinity without global freezes.

High-level id...

title: Real-world Inference: Mission & Positioning Brief
preview:
Purpose and audience:
This one-pager is for internal GTM, field, and product teams who need a concise, pragmatic story to explain why Redwood matters to engineers, platform teams, and technical buyers. Use this to align talk tracks for demos, early pitch decks, and onboarding for sales/solutions engineers.

Core mis...
text:
Purpose and audience:
This one-pager is for internal GTM, field, and product teams who need a concise, pragmatic story to explain why Redwood matters to engineers, platform teams, and technical buyers. Use this to align talk tracks for demos, early pitch decks, and onboarding for sales/solutions engineers.

Core mission statement (single sentence):
Make inference predictable, affordable, and operable so engineering teams can ship features rather than fight infrastructure.

Thesis (the why, in plain terms):
As models proliferate and hardware diversity explodes, competitive advantage moves from model selection to how reliably and cheaply you can serve model outputs at scale. Customers don’t buy models — they buy predictable latency, unit economics, and the ability to iterate without production regressions. Redwood is built around that reality: a unified runtime, observability, and polic...

text:
Purpose and scope
- Quick working draft: build a compact, parameterized spreadsheet and narrative that helps sales/product recommend a capacity-blend (steady-state on‑prem + burst VPC) and pick a licensing/support bundle to minimize blended unit cost while preserving SLA.
- Audience: sales engineering, product leads, solutions eng, finance.
- Status: exploratory model + sensitivity knobs; not final pricing.

Top-line hypothesis
- For sustained baseline throughput (>40% of peak) an amortized on‑prem footprint with a modest enterprise support bundle gives lower unit cost after ~18–30 months.
- For spiky or unpredictable workloads, hybrid (on‑prem baseline + VPC for peaks) reduces required on‑prem overprovisioning and often yields lower blended TCO when accounting for ops FTE and refresh risk.

Model structure (spreadsheet layout notes)

Purpose:
This checklist is a working draft intended for early-stage vendor security and privacy assessment when the vendor will host or operate LLM inference (hosted, dedicated, or private). Focus is on inference-specific risks: in-flight and cached context (KV/prefix caches), prompt/response logging, model provenance, and edge or private-hosted deployments.

Scope:
- Applies to third-party vendors and partners that will handle live inference traffic or provide managed inference runtime components.
- Not a replacement for full legal DPA negotiation; use as intake + evidence request to feed legal and procurement.

How to use:
1) Run intake call with vendor and fill the intake section below.
2) Request evidence items in parallel (technical screenshots, config excerpts, architecture diagrams).
3) Score risks and determine negotiation items.

text:
Purpose and scope:
This is a working lab journal capturing an incremental, empirical fingerprint of per-stage contributions to end-to-end response latency for streaming/incremental-output requests. Goal: record representative trace shapes (p50/p95/p99), coarse per-stage breakdowns, hypotheses for observed long-tail behaviors, and low-risk mitigations to iterate in the next sprint. This doc is intentionally informal — notes-to-self + triage trail for the perf triage meeting.

Context and motivation:
- Recent customer reports (ENG-2124) showed sporadic p99 spikes for chat-style streaming on a medium-sized model (1.3B-ish open model) when average sequence lengths were > 600 tokens.
- Baseline change: release v0.9.7 introduced a micro-optimization in KV cache metadata handling and expanded async-batcher windowing default from 20ms -> 30ms (PR 1125). Suspect interaction between larger batc...

preview:

- Observed: immediate small canar...
text:
Purpose: quick personal scratchpad for a breadcrumb-style canary that accounts for priming/lag in KV + cache layers and client SDK warmup. Not for copy-paste ops doc but to capture heuristics, signals, and open questions before formalizing into product playbook.

Background/context:
- Observed: immediate small canary traffic often shows degraded p95/p99 until prefix KV/caches hit. Some clients -> large initial tokens (long prompts) cause cold-run tail.
- Goal: reduce noisy false positives during early minutes; ensure canary growth only when steady-state metrics stable.

High-level plan (breadcrumb approach):
- Step 0: preflight checks (infra/telemetry) -> run smoke tests in staging (latency, errors, tokens)
- Step 1: anchor cohort (shadowing) -> 0.1% production traffic with responses only logged (no client routing) for 20m
- Step 2: breadcrumb cohort -> 0.5% routed, split across low-Q...

title: Research: Time skew in air-gapped environments (impact on offline bundle signature verification)
preview:
Last updated: 2026-01-18
Owner: Ethan Park

Context / why this doc exists
- We’re adding cryptographic signing + verification for Redwood Private offline bundles (air-gapped installs). Multiple pilot customers reported install failures caused by system clock drift ("certificate not yet valid" / validity checks faili...
text:
Last updated: 2026-01-18
Owner: Ethan Park

Context / why this doc exists
- We’re adding cryptographic signing + verification for Redwood Private offline bundles (air-gapped installs). Multiple pilot customers reported install failures caused by system clock drift ("certificate not yet valid" / validity checks failing even though the bundle is legitimate).
- Air-gapped environments often have strict time-source policies (no outbound NTP, sometimes no inbound NTP, sometimes "time is local responsibility" with manual changes).
- This doc collects what we’ve learned about time sources/constraints, proposes verification behavior (fail/allow/warn), and recommends defaults + diagnostics.

Scope
- Time skew realities in air-gapped / restricted networks.
- NTP/PTP restrictions and common time architectures.
- How signature validity windows should be handled for signed offline bundles.
- UX + ...

text:
Overview:

Quick working notes for Larkspur Telemed private deployment proof-of-concept and cutover. This doc captures sizing assumptions, a proposed phased upgrade/cutover plan, Terraform module callouts, networking/KMS considerations, and open questions. Intended audience: Solutions eng + infra + Larkspur infra lead (Ben K.).

Customer problem summary:
- Larkspur needs low-latency on-prem inference for clinical triage (chat + structured output) with strict PHI controls (no model telemetry offsite).
- Expected steady-state: 120 QPS text-gen (95th latency target < 120ms for short prompts), peak bursts up to 350 QPS for scheduled clinic hours.
- Throughput must support multi-model fallback (base model + smaller fallback for cost).

Constraints & hard requirements:
- Air-gapped deployment option required for production environment; control plane managed in customer VPC via private conne...

title: Cross-region fallback safety margin scenarios and activation checklist — Aug 2026
preview:
Purpose:
This working sheet captures a set of distinct cross-region fallback and safety-margin scenarios for GPU fleet capacity. The goal is pragmatic: drive a single operational rubric for when to (a) activate region-to-region failover, (b) bring up reserve pools, and (c) trigger customer-visible routing/degradatio...
text:
Purpose:
This working sheet captures a set of distinct cross-region fallback and safety-margin scenarios for GPU fleet capacity. The goal is pragmatic: drive a single operational rubric for when to (a) activate region-to-region failover, (b) bring up reserve pools, and (c) trigger customer-visible routing/degradation policies. This doc blends simple threshold rules with Monte Carlo–style scenario outcomes and a short activation checklist for on-call and SRE operators.

Executive summary (short):
- We recommend holding an operational buffer equivalent to 8–12% of steady-state peak GPU slots per cloud region as the baseline safety margin.
- For multi-region DR where cross-region failover is required, plan for an absorbable burst of up to 1.8x regional peak for up to 2 hours without service degradation by leveraging prioritized pre-warming + elastic burst pools.
- Activation is tiered: a...

title: Shadow Ratecard Calculator and Regional PPK Experiments
preview:

The goal is to produce actionable levers for hosted API pri...
text:
Purpose:
This sheet is a working calculator for creating a \"shadow\" ratecard (internal unit economics view) that synthesizes per-model, per-region COGS (PPK = price per 1k tokens), margin by latency tier, and a set of fallback/reserve routing experiments. The goal is to produce actionable levers for hosted API pricing, reserve capacity planning, and routing policies to protect margin during surges.

High-level assumptions and notes:
- Working draft: prioritized for speed and variant exploration, not audited GA numbers.
- Costs include GPU hourly amortized, instance networking egress (where applicable), license/amortization for model families, and a regional power/ops uplift.
- Token throughput estimates are empirically derived from perf traces (median tokens/sec per model family under 95th latency SLO).
- Fallbacks: when primary model unavailable, route to quantized variant or cheap...

title: cbatch oscillation debug log
preview:

Short session notes capturing commands tried, raw snippets, and working hypotheses for the intermittent throughput oscillations observed on node pool-rt-3 when continuous_batching is enabled. Goal is to preserve breadcrumbs for follow-up and handoff to SRE/infra.

- Starting 2025-10-29...
text:
Objective:
Short session notes capturing commands tried, raw snippets, and working hypotheses for the intermittent throughput oscillations observed on node pool-rt-3 when continuous_batching is enabled. Goal is to preserve breadcrumbs for follow-up and handoff to SRE/infra.

Summary of symptom:
- Starting 2025-10-29 we saw periodic ~40-70% drop in tokens/s for baseline text-generation workloads (seq_len=512, concurrency=40). Drops last 2–6 minutes then recover. Not correlated with GPU utilization spikes (GPU stays ~60–75%).
- Regression appears only when continuous_batching=true and kv_prefetch=true on the same runtime revision (serving-runtime:v2.14.1). No regression when we disable continuous_batching or pin to previous runtime v2.13.8.

Quick reproducer (local-ish):
1) Launch small cluster: kubectl apply -f test-cluster.yaml (k8s job using model-opt-7b)
2) Load generator: locust or...

title: Personal cutover playbook - quicknotes
preview:
Context: personal scratchpad for the scheduled cutover on Nov 5. Short, actionable checklist + my brain-dump. Not an official runbook; will copy items to the team runbook after validation.

Release summary:
- Target: rolling deploy of service@v2.14 across prod clusters (us-west1, eu-west1).

title: Backpressure and fairness playbook for mixed prefill/decode workloads
preview:
Purpose and scope:
This working playbook captures heuristics, control-loop designs, and experimental observations for enforcing backpressure and fairness across mixed prefill (context ingest + KV warmup) and decode (autoregressive token emission) streams. The goal is operational: keep tail latency for streaming resp...
text:
Purpose and scope:
This working playbook captures heuristics, control-loop designs, and experimental observations for enforcing backpressure and fairness across mixed prefill (context ingest + KV warmup) and decode (autoregressive token emission) streams. The goal is operational: keep tail latency for streaming responds under SLO while maximizing throughput and minimizing KV staleness regressions for long-running sessions.

Current problem statement (brief):
- In mixed workloads we see priority inversion and unfair slot-grabs: long prefill-heavy requests can occupy GPU memory/KV residency and starve latency-sensitive decodes.
- Naive admission (first-come microbatching) causes head-of-line blocking where large prefill microbatches delay smaller decode streams.
- Backpressure signals are noisy: wall-clock waiting time does not map cleanly to token emission urgency, and KV staleness inc...

title: Piecewise Parameter Stitching and mmap-Fence Hot-Swap Playbook
preview:
Purpose
This playbook describes a pragmatic design and operational pattern for: (1) streaming model parameters into GPU-resident memory in piecewise chunks, (2) using mmap-backed residency with an explicit fence mechanism to coordinate hot-swaps, and (3) ensuring KV cache and kernel scheduling remain consistent duri...
text:
Purpose
This playbook describes a pragmatic design and operational pattern for: (1) streaming model parameters into GPU-resident memory in piecewise chunks, (2) using mmap-backed residency with an explicit fence mechanism to coordinate hot-swaps, and (3) ensuring KV cache and kernel scheduling remain consistent during swaps. The goal is to reduce cold-start latency, avoid large synchronous IO pauses, and allow safe online model swaps for versioned rollouts.

Scope and non-goals
- Scope: tensor-level piecewise load, mmap residency semantics, safetensors streaming decode, hot-swap orchestration, KV cache invalidation/trim semantics, integrations with scheduler & kernel affinity hints.
- Non-goal: full replacement for external orchestration (k8s operators) or deeper model conversion tooling; we expect loaders to call out to existing conversion jobs when needed.

Constraints and assumptio...

title: Tenant Clockwork: Opportunistic Borrowing and Fallback PRD (draft)
preview:
Overview:

This document proposes Tenant Clockwork: an opportunistic borrowing and graceful fallback system for Dedicated pools. The motivation is to provide predictable latency SLOs for committed tenants while allowing low-friction, limited-capacity borrowing from underutilized committed capacity to absorb short sp...
text:
Overview:

This document proposes Tenant Clockwork: an opportunistic borrowing and graceful fallback system for Dedicated pools. The motivation is to provide predictable latency SLOs for committed tenants while allowing low-friction, limited-capacity borrowing from underutilized committed capacity to absorb short spikes. The mechanism prioritizes isolation and SLO safety, explicitly caps borrowing exposure, and includes a fast fallback path (graceful degradation + model variant routing) when borrowing cannot satisfy demand.

Context and problem statement:

- Dedicated customers pay for reserved GPU capacity with expectations of predictable throughput and latency. However, request patterns are bursty (microbursts, daily peaks, marketing events), and strict hard isolation leaves spare capacity unused for long periods, increasing overall cost for customers and for Redwood.
- Current appr...

title: Lightweight call reroute policy DSL (sketch)
preview:
Top-level goal: a tiny policy language (DSL) that client adapters can ship with to make fast, deterministic reroute/hedge decisions without pulling heavy config from control plane on every call.

Why:
- clients (edge proxies / lightweight sidecars) need predictable fallback behaviors when: regional capacity exhauste...
text:
Top-level goal: a tiny policy language (DSL) that client adapters can ship with to make fast, deterministic reroute/hedge decisions without pulling heavy config from control plane on every call.

Why:
- clients (edge proxies / lightweight sidecars) need predictable fallback behaviors when: regional capacity exhausted, model variant degraded, or transient infra errors (timeouts, 5xx).
- we want to avoid overloading the control plane and keep client behavior debuggable and auditable.
- policy must be small, explainable, and allow safe rollout of hedging and reroute without server-side changes.

Non-goals:
- replace server-side sophisticated routing; this is for conservative client decisions only.
- global policy orchestration (control plane will still distribute policies occasionally).

Requirements (informal):
1) Tiny syntax, ~10–30 lines typical.
2) Deterministic evaluation; no random...

title: Thermal surface and proactive degradation policy for mixed GPU fleets
preview:
Summary:
This doc defines a lightweight, empirically calibrated 'frequency surface' model and an operational policy for proactively degrading performance to respect GPU power/thermal envelopes across mixed fleets (A100/H100/L4/MI300). The goal is to keep tail latency and availability stable during thermal excursions...
text:
Summary:
This doc defines a lightweight, empirically calibrated 'frequency surface' model and an operational policy for proactively degrading performance to respect GPU power/thermal envelopes across mixed fleets (A100/H100/L4/MI300). The goal is to keep tail latency and availability stable during thermal excursions and mixed-workload microbursts while preserving unit-cost efficiency.

Motivation / Problem statement:
- We operate heterogeneous GPU pools with different thermal headroom and power efficiency. Workload microbursts and sustained high-util phases lead to thermal throttling or immediate frequency drops on some SKUs, which in turn causes latency amplification and instability for multi-tenant routing.
- Current reactive fallback (wait-for-throttle -> migrate -> cold start) is costly: high latencies, cache churn, and sometimes transient OOMs when KV caches are migrated.
- We ne...

title: Hosted Data Residency Enforcement — Implementation Plan (Engineering)
preview:
Context / TL;DR
We are delivering “Hosted data residency enforcement” for the Hosted API as part of the Enterprise Security & Compliance Foundation initiative. The work introduces customer-configurable region pinning (allowed region set + primary region), deterministic enforcement at request time (no cross-region ro...
text:
Context / TL;DR
We are delivering “Hosted data residency enforcement” for the Hosted API as part of the Enterprise Security & Compliance Foundation initiative. The work introduces customer-configurable region pinning (allowed region set + primary region), deterministic enforcement at request time (no cross-region routing/fallback unless explicitly allowed), and consistent error messaging + auditing.

This doc is the engineering execution plan (milestones, owners, dependencies, risks, and launch checklist). Source-of-truth for requirements + public contract is Confluence PRD + ADRs. This plan is intended to be updated as we learn during rollout.

Goals (engineering interpretation)
1) Deterministic request-time enforcement
- Given org/project/key residency policy, the system should either:
 a) route to a compliant region and continue, or
 b) block the request with a stable error contrac...

text:
Goal:
Build a small, re-usable catalog of latency "phenotypes" observed in our hosted workloads so we can map mitigations to categories instead of ad-hoc runs. This is a personal scratchpad — rough, experimental, lightweight cluster of notes + quick runs.

Why now:
- Several recent customer incidents map to different-looking tail shapes (one looked like a long plateau, another as fast tiny spikes).
- Want quick mapping from observed telemetry -> likely root cause / first mitigation to try.

Experiment summary (high level):
- Environment: dedicated dev cluster (g4dn-equivalent), single model (gpt-small-ish eval build), q8 quantized variant, 1 GPU per node.
- Client driver: locust-like harness (homegrown) producing Poisson arrivals and controlled bursts.
- Key knobs: concurrency (1, 4, 16), batch size cap (1, 8, 32), kv-cache primed vs cold, prefetch disabled vs enabled.

title: kernel interleaving replay labnote
preview:
Context and goal:

This is a running labnote capturing an odd micro-replay we observed in production traces: under mixed short+long sequence interleaving, tail latency sometimes jumps (p99 -> p99.9) for a subset of requests even though aggregate GPU utilization and kernel latencies look nominal. Hypothesis: patholog...
text:
Context and goal:

This is a running labnote capturing an odd micro-replay we observed in production traces: under mixed short+long sequence interleaving, tail latency sometimes jumps (p99 -> p99.9) for a subset of requests even though aggregate GPU utilization and kernel latencies look nominal. Hypothesis: pathological kernel interleaving between FlashAttention and a paged-attention fallback (or fused op boundary) causes occasional path-dependent stalls / extra synchronizations.

Why this matters:
- Customer-facing inference latency SLOs are sensitive to 99.9 percentiles; intermittent micro-replays create large error budgets for streaming/chat workloads.
- If the issue is deterministic/structural (scheduler + kernel handoff) we can mitigate at runtime without model changes.

High-level observations (from traces):
- Repro window: observed in two different clusters (g5x2-equivalent) wi...

title: log-domain-kv-representation-prototype
preview:
Summary:

This note captures a lightweight prototype and design rationale for storing and updating KV cache tensors in a log-domain (magnitude + exponent) representation rather than raw floating point. The motivating problem is intermittent underflow/precision loss and NaN propagation observed with mixed-precision (...
text:
Summary:

This note captures a lightweight prototype and design rationale for storing and updating KV cache tensors in a log-domain (magnitude + exponent) representation rather than raw floating point. The motivating problem is intermittent underflow/precision loss and NaN propagation observed with mixed-precision (FP8/FP16) kernels when long-prefix caching and repeated accumulation occur. We explore a hybrid log-space representation that reduces dynamic-range driven losses while minimizing runtime cost and compatibility friction with our serving runtime.

Problem statement / motivation:

- In production traces with long-context prompts (2k+ tokens) we see rare but actionable quality regression and NaN events tied to KV accumulation and subsequent attention softmax normalization.
- Mixed-precision kernels (FP8 with FP16 accumulators, or bfloat16 paths) can lose magnitude information f...

title: Prefetch strategies and local tool residency (WIP)
preview:
Executive summary:

This WIP explores a combined runtime+SDK approach for lowering perceived latency for common tool-calling workflows by using coordinated prefetch windows, lightweight session residency for frequently used tool state ("tool retainer"), and compact resumption frames for interrupted streaming session...
text:
Executive summary:

This WIP explores a combined runtime+SDK approach for lowering perceived latency for common tool-calling workflows by using coordinated prefetch windows, lightweight session residency for frequently used tool state ("tool retainer"), and compact resumption frames for interrupted streaming sessions. The focus is on hosted API ergonomics: how the server advertises prefetch hints and tool residency policies, and how client SDKs manage cached tool handles, pre-executed partial results, and resumable streams while preserving correctness and safety.

Why this problem matters:
- Many integrations (calendar lookup, contact fetch, code search) are high-value but have deterministic, short-latency operations that dominate user-perceived latency.
- Existing streaming approaches wait for on-demand tool calls or run synchronous wrap-around calls that add 100-700ms to the first a...

text:
Purpose
---
Notes from ad-hoc profiling focused on CPU-side hotspots in the serving path: tokenization, request parsing, thread pools, and Python runtime overhead. Goal: find low-friction mitigations that reduce 95th and median latency for small-text requests (seq_len ~ 64-256) without touching model kernels.

Background / environment
- Service: FastAPI (uvicorn), async handlers, tokenization done in a ThreadPoolExecutor to avoid blocking event loop.
- Model backend: local C++ runtime (RPC-ish) invoked via Python bindings.
- Test workload: single-turn prompt generation, batch sizes 1-4, 1000 requests steady state, synthetic variety of input lengths.
- Baseline machine: 32 vCPU (Intel), 8 logical cores dedicated to workers in k8s node.

Quick profiler summary (perf + py-spy samples):
- Tokenization (Python glue + sentencepiece Python wrapper): ~28% of sampled CPU time.
- JSON parsing /...

title: Compute capability mismatch - fp16 rounding causing hallucinations (postmortem draft)
preview:
Summary:
We observed a small but user-visible spike in hallucinations and truncated reasoning chains after a canary rollout of model-variant v2.1. The canary ran on a small zone of instances with older GPUs (compute capability 6.1) while the rest of the fleet uses compute capability 8.6. The root cause appears to be...
text:
Summary:
We observed a small but user-visible spike in hallucinations and truncated reasoning chains after a canary rollout of model-variant v2.1. The canary ran on a small zone of instances with older GPUs (compute capability 6.1) while the rest of the fleet uses compute capability 8.6. The root cause appears to be subtle fp16 rounding behavior differences in older SM architecture leading to repeated token sampling instability for long-context prompts.

Impact:
- Time window: 2025-02-18 03:12 -> 2025-02-18 05:45 UTC
- Affected traffic: ~1.3% of Hosted API requests (canary routing)
- Symptom: increased hallucination rate on long (>=1024 token) chains and early-eos or unexpected repetition in ~4.2% of requests in canary slice (baseline ~0.4%).
- Downstream: Two customer incidents reported webhook failures due to truncated JSON in responses (codec errors).

How it was detected:
- Automa...

text:
Quick scratchpad: token alignment weirdness seen in nightly run (2025-02-13 -> 2025-02-14). Putting raw notes here before a proper ticket write-up. Mostly heuristics + examples + quick experiments to try.

Context:
- Observed lower quality scores on 'structured extraction' evals after tokenizer merge (vocab-merge-v2) rolled in nightly. Regression magnitude: 3–6 points absolute on small eval set (n=120).
- Affected flows: CSV->JSON extraction, instruction-following prompts where output required stable tokens (IDs, short alphanum codes).
- Hypothesis: tokenization changes cause off-by-one token splits, which then interact poorly with KV cache boundaries and early-stop heuristics in continuous-batching path.

Ad-hoc rubric (for quick human spot checks) — 1 page, compact scoring:
- 4 = Perfect: extracted fields present, separators intact, tokenization preserved (no mid-token splits), exac...

title: Auto-batcher heuristics debug
preview:

Summary:

text:
Scratchpad: auto-batcher behaviour investigation (misc notes + commands run)

Summary:
- Nightly regression: p50 token throughput dropped ~18% for chat endpoint between 2025-02-11 02:00 & 02:30 UTC. No model change. Traffic distribution shifted slightly (more short-turn prompts), but not enough to account for drop. Hypothesis: continuous-batcher heuristic misfired (thresholds or temporal decay) causing smaller batches + KV-cache thrash.

What I saw in metrics (quick):
- baseline (2025-02-11 01:50): p50 tokens/sec ~ 3200, p95 latency 120ms
- regression window (02:05–02:25): p50 tokens/sec ~ 2600, p95 latency 160ms
- runtime CPU on hosts jumped ~12% during window, queue length increased modestly
- observed cache miss rate for prefix cache jumped from 4% -> 21% (sampling from /metrics/kvcache/miss_rate)

Commands I ran (local):
- tail recent logs (pod): kubectl -n serving-runtime logs -f...

Purpose:

Capture design notes for customer POCs where Redwood Private is deployed across a hybrid footprint: customer VPC(s) + Redwood-managed VPC(s) (could be cross-account). Goal is to define a repeatable service boundary pattern (ingress, egress, DNS, endpoints, routing, and failover) that balances security (least privilege, auditability) and operational simplicity for short POC timelines.

Background / constraints:
- Target customers: regulated SaaS or fintech customers running an on-prem or customer-VPC control plane with inference nodes in Redwood-managed VPC or partner cloud account.
- Typical ask: private inference connectivity, no public egress for model artifacts/keys, predictable SNAT behavior, and split-horizon DNS so hostnames resolve to private endpoints.
- Timebox: POC must be deployable in 1–2 days for standard templates, 3–5 days for custom networking.

title: Stochastic sched + KV fuzz experiment diary
preview:
Summary:
This is a working diary of a set of experiments that combine probabilistic/sampled scheduling heuristics with aggressive KV-cache churn fuzzing. The goal: surface brittle codepaths in the scheduler + kernel selection logic that only trigger under realistic but low-frequency trace patterns. This doc collects...
text:
Summary:
This is a working diary of a set of experiments that combine probabilistic/sampled scheduling heuristics with aggressive KV-cache churn fuzzing. The goal: surface brittle codepaths in the scheduler + kernel selection logic that only trigger under realistic but low-frequency trace patterns. This doc collects test designs, microbench workloads, quick results, and regression artifacts for follow-up.

Motivation / Why this feels urgent:
- Multiple customer incidents (late-2024) triggered by rare sequences: long context prefixes + intermittent small requests caused the KV cache to thrash and the scheduler to choose high-latency kernels.
- Our existing probebench seeding covers common shapes but misses low-probability prefix reuse and interleaving patterns seen in production.
- We need a repeatable harness that stitches real traces with synthetic perturbations and stress-schedules ...

title: Microburst credit pools and soft preemption: PRD sketch
preview:
Context

This is a working sketch for a dedicated-capacity feature set that aims to let tenants absorb short, high-concurrency demand spikes without large long-term commit increases while preserving tenant isolation and predictable SLOs. Unlike hard evictions or purely queue-based throttles, the idea is a two-tier c...
text:
Context

This is a working sketch for a dedicated-capacity feature set that aims to let tenants absorb short, high-concurrency demand spikes without large long-term commit increases while preserving tenant isolation and predictable SLOs. Unlike hard evictions or purely queue-based throttles, the idea is a two-tier credit + soft-preemption model that gracefully degrades noncritical capacity, shifts to cheaper model variants, and uses shadow pools for observed microbursts.

Problem statement

- Customers want predictable latency and capacity for steady traffic but also need to support occasional high-concurrency bursts (cron spikes, flash events, promotion traffic).
- Buying large reserved capacity for rare bursts is expensive and underutilized.
- Current approaches (strict tenant quotas, aggressive queuing, or hard eviction) cause poor UX or noisy neighbor concerns.

Design goals

1) A...

title: region-aware-sku-variability-and-cache-thresholds
preview:

Quick working notes from the cross-region/cross-sku runs we kicked off to understand how driver + CUDA + region placement interact with cache thresholds and observed tail noise. Goal: find stable config knobs for Dedicated and Private deployments (default kernel + cache thresholds) that generalize across A...
text:
Summary:

Quick working notes from the cross-region/cross-sku runs we kicked off to understand how driver + CUDA + region placement interact with cache thresholds and observed tail noise. Goal: find stable config knobs for Dedicated and Private deployments (default kernel + cache thresholds) that generalize across AWS p4/p5 and GCP L4/compute SKUs.

What we ran (matrix):
- SKUs: AWS p4d (A100), AWS p5 (H100), GCP a2/a2-megagpu (A100 variants), GCP l4d (L40S).
- CUDA/driver combos: CUDA 11.8 / NVIDIA driver 535.xx, CUDA 12.2 / driver 545.xx, and a legacy 470.xx on A100 for historical comparison.
- Workloads: 8k-context autoregressive generation, batch sizes {1, 4, 8}, pipelined streaming (1 token at a time) vs batched token append, KV-cache enabled vs disabled, warm caches vs cold-start, and long-concat (incremental context growth up to 32k).
- Metrics captured: p50/p95/p99 per-token l...

Quick, tactical playbook to help SDRs, AEs, and Solutions Engineers tailor short talk tracks and demo hooks to three overlapping engineering personas: ML engineers, backend engineers, and platform teams. This is intentionally compact — meant for call prep and one-pagers, not the canonical persona library.

text:
Purpose:
Quick, tactical playbook to help SDRs, AEs, and Solutions Engineers tailor short talk tracks and demo hooks to three overlapping engineering personas: ML engineers, backend engineers, and platform teams. This is intentionally compact — meant for call prep and one-pagers, not the canonical persona library.

TL;DR:
- ML engineers care about model fidelity, reproducibility, latency for interactive workflows, and tooling to iterate on prompts/evals.
- Backend engineers care about predictable latency, simple API semantics, and cost per token/unit under load.
- Platform teams care about multi-tenant controls, rollout/upgrade automation, observability, and procurement/SLA details.

Positioning thesis (one line each):
- ML engineer: \"Ship higher-quality LLM features faster by giving you a reproducible serving environment and first-class evals so model regressions become a solved wor...
